decks.description: This deck is a mono-white 'attack alone' aggro deck that wants to send a single creature into combat each turn and shower it with bonuses rather than swinging wide. Peggy Carter, Secret Agent, Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D., Strategic Intervention, and Nick Fury, Spymaster all key off a creature attacking alone, granting indestructibility, +1/+1, tapping down blockers, and even letting you cheat an extra creature onto the battlefield tapped and attacking. Agent 13, Sharon Carter turns that same lone attacker into card advantage via Clue tokens, while Agent Phil Coulson stacks permanent +1/+1 counters onto your other Heroes to make that solo attacker (or its backups) hit progressively harder. The synergy is explicit: Nick Fury's trigger draws a card and can put a cheap creature onto the battlefield tapped and attacking, which itself can then trigger the other attack-alone payoffs like Agent 13's investigate or Strategic Intervention's tap effect, chaining value off a single combat step. S.H.I.E.L.D. Helicarrier and Borough Backup provide bodies and crew fodder, while Helicarrier Strike and Web Up offer removal/exile to clear the way for your solo attacker to connect unimpeded.

This is a mono-white lifegain deck that converts incidental life totals into growing threats and battlefield-wide buffs. MJ, Rising Star gets a +1/+1 counter every time you gain life, while Doctor Strange, Surgeon doubles all life gained and triggers a team-wide +2/+2 and vigilance buff once you're 10 life above your starting total, making every lifegain source (Valkyrior Skyrider's 4 life, Night Nurse's lifelink, Infinity Formula's combat lifegain, Fall to Earth's 3 life) compound into a late-game overrun. Doctor Jane Foster synergizes directly with the lifegain theme by returning a cheap creature from the graveyard to your hand, or straight to the battlefield if you gained life that turn, letting you recur fallen threats like Night Nurse or MJ for free. Take Up the Shield and Crowd of True Believers reinforce solo-attacker survivability and incremental lifegain, while Super Villain Lockup and Web Up-style exile effects (here Fall to Earth) handle problem threats, all building toward a slow grind where life totals become a resource that snowballs into bigger creatures and a devastating alpha strike.

This white counters-and-value deck centers on protecting and growing a board of +1/+1 counter creatures while grinding card advantage through ETB triggers. Matt Murdock, Justice Seeker gives every countered creature ward 1, making your board resilient to removal, and Costume Closet stores counters that redistribute onto creatures as they die or enter, feeding Political Triumph's scry-and-plan-counter engine which eventually draws a card and buffs the whole team. The clearest combo is She-Hulk, Attorney-at-Law's power-up ability doubling every +1/+1 counter on your board, which turns modest investments from Foggy Nelson, Daily Bugle Reporters, or Selfless Police Captain's leave-the-battlefield trigger into an explosive late-game swing. Removal comes from exile effects like Web Up, Vault Guardsman's ETB exile, and Murdock's Crusade's teamwork-scaling exile of big or expensive permanents, letting the deck stall behind incremental value until it can double its counters and close the game.

This is a mono-white token-and-counters deck built around generating a wide board of Soldiers and Heroes that scale together. Okoye, Dora Milaje Leader and Wakandan Shield Guard flood the board with 1/1 Soldier tokens, and Okoye's static ability gives all attacking creature tokens first strike, meaning even small tokens can profitably trade up in combat. Black Panther, Most Dangerous reflects damage back at any target when he's hit, punishing opponents who try to block or burn him, and his power-up ability pumps every other creature by +2/+2 for a game-ending alpha strike once you have the mana. Ultimate Alliance scales its damage with your total creature count, making it a natural finisher after Okoye and Shield Guard have gone wide, while Shuri, Vibranium Technologist and Secure Detention's token side-benefit keep adding bodies, and Vibranium Energy Daggers plus Panther Pounce give the deck ways to push extra damage or draw cards off Clue tokens.

This mono-white deck is built explicitly around the Battalion mechanic, wanting to attack with three or more creatures simultaneously to trigger powerful team-wide bonuses from General Thunderbolt Ross, Falcon, Joaquin Torres, and Captain America, Skybound all at once. Captain America, Skybound's Battalion trigger puts a +1/+1 counter on every attacking creature and grants them indestructible, while General Thunderbolt Ross's Battalion gives all attackers +1/+0, so a single wide swing with these three online turns a modest board into a nearly unkillable alpha strike. Falcon adds card selection to the mix via scry 1 on his own Battalion trigger. Supporting pieces like Wakandan Shield Guard, U.S.Agent (who arrives with a free Equipment already attached), and War Machine's attack-tapper help ensure enough bodies survive to attack together, while Helicarrier Strike, Heroic Teamwork, and Ultimate Alliance provide combat tricks and removal that key off having multiple creatures already committed to the board.

This deck's engine is entirely about Equipment support, using cheap gear to power up a small number of key creatures rather than going wide. Captain America, Liberator tutors an Equipment with mana value 3 or less straight onto the battlefield when he enters, and then creates a 1/1 Soldier token for every Equipment attached to him whenever he attacks, directly rewarding you for loading him up with Captain America's Shield, Infinity Formula, or a Sturdy Shield token. U.S.Agent, John Walker and Origin of Captain America both generate their own free Sturdy Shield tokens, giving you multiple equip targets without spending extra equip costs, while Patriot, Young Avenger gets a team-wide +1/+0 anthem simply for being equipped himself. Peggy Carter and Agent 13, Sharon Carter reward attacking alone with indestructibility and Clue-token card draw, giving the deck a secondary lone-attacker plan alongside its Equipment payoffs, and Bucky Barnes, Eager Ally's death trigger digs specifically for more Equipment, Heroes, or Soldiers to keep the engine running.

This white deck is a counters-matter tempo build where nearly every spell that targets your own creatures triggers permanent growth on key threats. Colleen Wing, Street Samurai, Hawkeye, Bowslinger, and Mockingbird, Ace Agent all put a +1/+1 counter on themselves whenever you cast a spell targeting a creature you control, so cheap combat tricks like Panther Pounce, Stunning Shot, and Heroic Teamwork do double duty as both tempo plays and permanent stat boosts, with Colleen Wing and Hawkeye additionally scrying to smooth draws. Stunning Shot exemplifies the deck's dual-purpose design by both adding two counters to your creature and stunning down an opposing blocker in one card. Prowess creatures like Agent of Atlas and Kree Commandos passively grow alongside this targeted-spell density, while Sudden Strike and Fall to Earth provide removal to clear the way for your steadily-counter-laden creatures to close out the game.

This white deck leans on the Web-slinging mechanic, letting you cheat Spider-Man, Web-Slinger, Spider-UK, and Amazing Spider-Girl into play cheaply by bouncing a tapped creature back to hand instead of paying full cost, which combos naturally with ETB-heavy creatures you want to replay anyway. Aunt May turns every creature entering into incidental lifegain and pumps Spiders specifically with a +1/+1 counter, so bouncing and recasting a Spider via web-slinging both re-triggers its ETB and grows it further under Aunt May. Spider-UK's own ability rewards this bounce-and-replay plan directly, drawing a card and gaining 2 life at end step if two or more creatures entered that turn, which is easy to hit when web-slinging naturally doubles up ETB triggers. Spider-Mobile scales its combat stats with your Spider count, and Flash Thompson's flash-in tap/untap flexibility along with Thwip!'s Spider-specific lifegain and buff round out a deck that wants a steady stream of creatures entering the battlefield rather than a big single bomb.

This white deck is built around blink-and-exile effects that generate repeatable value and life swings, centered on Captain Marvel, Shooting Star and Photon, Lady of Light. Captain Marvel exiles a creature on ETB or attack (with the controller gaining life equal to its power, but you gaining that same life back from her second ability whenever any other creature is exiled), while Photon's attack trigger flickers another one of your own creatures, letting you re-trigger ETB abilities like Ms. Marvel's combat-damage buff, Captain Marvel's own exile trigger, or Marvel Boy's counter-growth. Quantum Entanglement doubles as a mini blink engine, letting you flicker a creature at end step for repeated ETB value, and combined with Captain Mar-Vell's flash-everything ability during opponents' turns, the deck can generate surprise value at instant speed. Ms. Marvel, Elastic Ally rewards any of these temporary power boosts by drawing a card when a pumped creature connects, tying the flicker and pump effects together into a card-advantage engine.

This deck is a Hero tribal build that leans on the exalted mechanic to punish opponents when a lone creature attacks, alongside broad Hero-count synergies. Zarda, the Power Princess grants every other Hero exalted, so with several Heroes on board (Doctor Spectrum, Hero in Training, Nighthawk, Dark Defender, Hyperion, Supreme Hero), attacking alone stacks up multiple +1/+1 bonuses at once, turning a single attacker into a serious threat, while Nighthawk's own ETB and attack trigger adds even more pump to whichever creature you're sending in. Doctor Spectrum's modal ETB can put a +1/+1 counter on every other Hero you control, directly feeding the same exalted-boosted attacker plan, and Hyperion's damage-prevention shield protects your Heroes from being raced. Blur of Heroism lets you flash in Hero spells as surprise blockers or combat tricks, while Hyperion's Atomic Vision and Web Up provide removal, rounding out a deck whose gameplan is assembling a wide Hero board but often winning through a single, heavily-buffed exalted attacker.

This is a teamwork-matters mono-blue control/tempo deck built around tapping creatures to pay teamwork costs for bigger payoffs. Cards like We Say Thee Nay!, Timeline Inquiry, Atlantis Attacks, and Quantum Reduction all have Teamwork X, meaning tapping enough creature power upgrades the counterspell to a harder-to-pay one, turns Timeline Inquiry into card draw without discard, gives Atlantis Attacks both modes at once (making a 6/5 hexproof Leviathan token AND bouncing permanents), and lets Quantum Reduction be cast at flash speed. The creature base itself rewards casting noncreature spells: TVA Bureaucrat and Machine Man, Model X-51 both grow and gain evasion whenever you cast a noncreature spell, while Vision, Spectral Synthezoid lets you cast one such spell free each turn, effectively fueling those triggers repeatedly. Echo, Perceptive Prodigy can copy a triggered/activated ability from another creature (like Viv Vision's power-up or Victor Mancha's exile trigger), adding some flexible value. Overall the deck plays a grindy tempo-control game, tapping down creatures for value spells while sneaking in evasive threats and locking down opposing creatures with Frozen in Ice.

This mono-blue deck is built entirely around the connive mechanic (draw then discard, and if you toss a nonland card, put a +1/+1 counter on the creature), triggering it constantly through combat and ETB effects: Mob Lookout, Tiger Shark, Abyssal Hunter, Doc Ock's Henchmen, Unstable Experiment, Trickster's Stratagem, and Namor-style Leader, Super-Genius all cause connives on entry, attack, or at the beginning of combat. Leader, Super-Genius doubles the value by making any connive draw an extra card first, while Kid Loki and Graviton, Fundamental Force both key off drawing your second card each turn (which naturally happens from repeated connives), growing Kid Loki with counters and letting Graviton grant flying or tap down blockers. Doc Ock, Sinister Scientist becomes an 8/8 once your graveyard (filled by all that discarding) hits eight cards, and gains hexproof as long as you control another Villain, making him a resilient finisher fueled directly by the deck's own discard engine. The overall plan is a value-grindy tempo deck that filters draws into permanent stat growth while incidentally filling the graveyard to power up its biggest threat.

This mono-blue deck leans heavily on drawing extra cards to fuel its 'second card each turn' and hand-size payoffs, playing a card-advantage-driven midrange game. Kid Loki and Blue Marvel, Adam Brashear both put +1/+1 counters on themselves whenever you draw your second card in a turn, which is easy to trigger given how many cantrips and draw effects are packed in (Fantastic Bounce, Futurist Forge, Super Intelligence, Bold Biochemist's power-up). Brawn, Amadeus Cho draws a card on entry and can later dump enormous counters onto himself equal to your hand size via his power-up, directly rewarded by Reed Richards, Smartest Man's ability that turns most of your draws into four-card draws, letting you stockpile a huge hand to then unload into Brawn's power-up for a massive one-shot growth spell. Ant-Man, Reformed Rogue and Beast, Erudite Aerialist both convert combat damage into more card draw, keeping the engine self-sustaining, while Frozen in Ice and Super Intelligence provide interaction/control pieces to buy time for the draw engine to take over.

This mono-blue artifact-matters deck plays a value/tempo game centered on bounce effects, ETB triggers, and Iron Man, Bleeding Edge's ability to copy artifact spells you cast (except the copy isn't legendary), which pairs well with a deck full of impactful artifacts like Iron Suitcase, Collector's Case, and Futurist Forge, each doubling their ETB value (extra scry, extra tap-and-stun, extra card draw) when doubled. Rescue, Pepper Potts bounces your own artifact or creature on entry and gets a +1/+1 counter if it targeted an artifact, letting you replay ETB effects like S.H.I.E.L.D. Deployment Drone's Soldier token or the artifacts above for repeated value. Origin of Iron Man ties the whole plan together as a Saga that locks down a creature, draws two cards, and finally lets you cheat an artifact with mana value 5 or less straight onto the battlefield, essentially free-casting Iron Man himself or another key artifact. The deck otherwise plays a tempo-control shell with flyers like War Machine and Iron Lad providing evasive damage while bounce and taplock effects like Happy Hogan and Collector's Case slow the opponent down.

This is a Merfolk tribal deck in blue built around the connive mechanic and 'draw your second card' triggers to grow a wide team of fish creatures. Attuma, Atlantean Warlord pumps all other Merfolk and draws a card whenever one or more Merfolk attack, synergizing directly with token generators like Daughter of the Deep (which makes a 1/1 Merfolk whenever you draw your second card each turn) and Namora, the Sea Queen's power-up that makes two more Merfolk tokens, quickly filling the board with attackers that all trigger Attuma's card draw. Namor, Scourge of the Seas gives flying to any Merfolk with a +1/+1 counter, which combines naturally with the deck's many connive effects (Atlantean Skirmisher, Namor's own combat trigger, Unstable Experiment) that put counters on creatures, turning a swarm of ground Merfolk into an evasive flying army. Atlantis Attacks and Quantum Reduction round out the deck with Teamwork-cost spells that scale up when you tap enough Merfolk power, letting the tribal swarm double as a mana sink for bigger removal or a giant Leviathan token.

This mono-blue deck's core plan is repeatedly bouncing and blinking its own permanents to re-trigger enter-the-battlefield abilities, most explicitly through Silver Surfer, Cosmic Voyager, who exiles any number of your other permanents and returns them at end of turn, resetting ETBs like S.H.I.E.L.D. Deployment Drone's token creation or Shipwreck Patrol's tap-and-stun. Justice, Vance Astrovik both bounces a nonland permanent on entry and grows with a +1/+1 counter whenever another nonland permanent you control is returned to hand, meaning Shi'ar Soldier's repeatable self-bounce ability or Silver Surfer's mass exile-and-return effect directly feeds Justice's counters over and over. Victor Timely, Wily Tycoon lets you recast a cheap instant/sorcery from the graveyard for free, and Spaceshift exiles and returns a permanent with a +1/+1 counter attached, giving more blink-style value alongside a suite of counterspells (We Say Thee Nay!, Dismissive Denial) and tap effects (Collector's Case) that lock down the board while the blink engine snowballs.

This mono-blue deck focuses on shrinking and resizing creatures through Pym Particles-style effects to unlock evasive attacks and card draw. Wasp, Shrinking Savior shrinks a creature by -3/-0 when she attacks and then draws a card for every creature on the battlefield with negative power, which combos directly with Depower (which gives -4/-0 to an attacking creature, cheaper if it's attacking, and cantrips) — stacking these effects can push multiple creatures below zero power simultaneously, maximizing Wasp's card draw. Ant-Man, Reformed Rogue also profits from the deck's spread of blue and green-costed spells, shrinking in power but becoming unblockable on blue spells while drawing cards off combat damage. Robotics Mastery adds two 1/1 flying Robot tokens on top of a combat trick, while Giant-Sized Flying Ant and Pym Particles itself provide flash tempo plays (tapping down blockers or making an attacker unblockable) that keep the size-shifting theme consistent across removal, tempo, and value pieces.

This is a mono-blue flyers-tribal deck whose engine centers on Vulture, Feathered Fiend, who puts a +1/+1 counter on every flying creature that connects with combat damage and draws you a card for the group, rewarding a wide board of evasive attackers like Aerial Doombot, S.H.I.E.L.D. Deployment Drone, and Falcon, Winged Wonder (whose Redwing token also flies and surveils on attack). Flying Drone's looter ability gets cheaper specifically when another flyer entered that turn, meaning the deck's steady stream of flying ETB creatures (S.H.I.E.L.D. Deployment Drone, Falcon's Redwing token, Justice) actively discounts its own card selection engine. Falcon's Wing Harness grants flying and ward to a nonflier, letting you funnel more attackers into the Vulture-fueled counter/draw loop, while Whoosh! and I Am Iron Man supply extra tempo and card advantage to smooth the curve as the flying team grows turn over turn.

This mono-blue deck is a spells-and-card-draw control shell that rewards drawing extra cards each turn and cheaply recasting past spells. Immortus, Master of Eternity taps for extra blue mana equal to cards drawn that turn (spendable only on noncreature spells) and his power-up wheels every player's hand and graveyard into a fresh seven-card draw, synergizing with a deck full of card-advantage engines like Bold Biochemist's power-up, Think Twice's flashback, and Timeline Inquiry's Teamwork-scaled card draw, all of which help fill Immortus's mana pool before he refills everyone's hands. TVA Bureaucrat and Pharaoh Rama-Tut both benefit directly from a noncreature-spell-heavy build: Bureaucrat pumps and becomes unblockable, while Rama-Tut connives, growing and filtering cards whenever a noncreature spell resolves. Victor Timely, Wily Tycoon and Multiversal Recruitment's flashback let you recur cheap spells or clone creatures for extra value, and Time Warp caps it all off by giving you an additional turn to capitalize on the card-advantage lead the deck has built.

This mono-blue copy-and-clone deck plays around turning creatures into copies of other permanents to maximize value from strong ETB or ability-based effects. Loki, Lord of Misrule can turn every other creature you control into a copy of one chosen creature (losing legendary status), which combos powerfully with Impossible Man, letting you first turn Impossible Man into a copy of a strong permanent and then use Loki to convert your whole board into copies of that same creature. Multiversal Recruitment (with its flashback) and The Clone Saga's chapter II both let you copy a creature spell as it's cast, stacking additional clone value on top of Loki's board-wide copy effect. Kid Loki and connive-based value (A.I.M. Scientists, Trickster's Stratagem) round out the deck's card-filtering plan, while Ghost, Spectral Saboteur provides an unblockable evasive clock and We Say Thee Nay! and Quantum Reduction offer interaction to protect the combo pieces until they can take over the game.

This mono-black deck wants you to attack with a single creature each turn and reap disproportionate rewards for it. Bob, Reluctant HYDRA Agent bounces himself back to hand after attacking alone to drain 2 life, Viper, Cruel Conspirator can pump or grant deathtouch/lifelink specifically to a lone attacker, and HYDRA Infiltration drains 1 life whenever any creature you control attacks alone, so the deck is built around solo swings rather than wide boards. Alongside that theme is a steady stream of 2/1 menace Villain tokens: Agents of HYDRA leaves one behind when it dies, HYDRA Troopers and Arnim Zola, Bio-Fanatic make one once your graveyard has two-plus creatures, and HYDRA Disintegrator both makes and instantly equips one of these tokens. Baron Strucker, HYDRA Overlord ties it together by discounting Villain spells and letting any newly entered Villain connive, which digs for gas while growing your board, and Crossbones, Malicious Mercenary explicitly triggers off those same Villain entries to burn opponents for 2 and grow himself. Removal like Dark Deed and Cruel Alliance (with its Teamwork alternate mode for a straight exile plus life gain) rounds out an otherwise grindy black attrition shell.

This is a tribal Villain deck that leans into recursive value and slow inevitability. The Masters of Evil itself pumps every other Villain you control by +2/+1, turning even small tokens into real threats, while Baron-Strucker-style entering triggers cascade: Yellowjacket, Heartless Marauder gets bigger and gains lifelink whenever another Villain enters, and Crimson Cowl, Master of Evil spits out a 2/1 menace Villain token whenever your nontoken Villains attack, feeding both Yellowjacket's trigger and the tribal buff. Villainous Syndication slowly mills itself into a battlefield reanimation, tapping Villains to add plan counters until the fourth returns a creature from the graveyard, giving the deck a long-game recursive finisher. Titania, Rugged Rumbler and its ward tax, plus Radioactive Man's brutal half-life drain on combat damage, provide hard-to-remove attackers, while Klaw, Sonic Subjugator and Hour of Defeat handle disruption and removal. Overall this is a grindy value pile that wants to go wide with cheap Villains, buff them all at once, and out-resource the opponent rather than execute one specific combo kill.

This deck is a straightforward black reanimator/graveyard-value pile that wants to fill its own graveyard early and then repeatedly cheat creatures back onto the battlefield. Doom's Servo-Guards and Venomized-style mill effects (via Arnim Zola and other self-mill) stock the graveyard, and then engines like Grim Reaper, Lethal Legionnaire (reanimate a creature tapped and attacking when he attacks), Too Evil to Stay Dead (a Teamwork spell that reanimates any creature card, or a 4-or-less creature without teamwork), and Grim Reaper's Scythe (which sacrifices two creatures to reanimate one with a finality counter, and also makes a 2/2 Zombie whenever creatures leave your graveyard) all turn your graveyard into a second hand. Undead Hand Ninja explicitly triggers whenever creature cards leave your graveyard, draining opponents and gaining you life every time you reanimate, so it directly synergizes with Grim Reaper's Scythe and Grim Reaper's attack trigger for repeated drain. Ultimo, Civilization's End adds a forced-sacrifice angle both on ETB and by discard, while Infernal Rebirth and Unliving Legionnaire's power-up give extra ways to rebuy creatures, making this a resilient attrition deck built to out-value opponents over time.

This deck is built around a sacrifice-and-Treasure engine that converts expendable bodies into card advantage and mana. Common Crook and Agents of HYDRA both leave value (a Treasure or a 2/1 menace token) when they die, feeding sacrifice outlets like Hammerhead, Maggia Boss (sac to grow), Kingpin's Enforcers (sac to draw), and especially Deadly Dispute, which sacrifices a creature or artifact to draw two cards and make a Treasure—refilling the resources it just spent. Kingpin, Wilson Fisk turns any sacrifice (including of himself) into two Treasures once per turn, which can immediately fuel Count Nefaria's cost reduction, since Nefaria costs {3} less if you've sacrificed a permanent that turn, letting you cheat out a flying 8/8-class threat early. Madame Masque's connive plus her second-draw-of-the-turn trigger (making a menace token) combines naturally with the deck's card-draw outlets like Deadly Dispute and Kingpin's Enforcers, since drawing extra cards from sacrifice effects can trigger a second token that turn. Overall it's an aggressive, grindy sacrifice deck with Villain-cost discounts from Tombstone and Criminal Enterprise's death-trigger drain rounding out the plan.

This is an Equipment-matters aggro deck where creatures want to carry hardware into combat for direct payoff. Whiplash, Vengeful Engineer literally drains life equal to the number of Equipment attached to him when he attacks, so stacking multiple Equipment (Stolen Stark Tech, Voltaic Whip, Swordsman's Steel, HYDRA Disintegrator) onto him turns into a snowballing life-swing threat. Swordsman, Sharp Scoundrel automatically attaches an Equipment to a creature whenever another Villain enters, essentially giving you free equip triggers, and any equipped creature he triggers also connives when it attacks, digging for more gear or growing itself. Ronin, Shadow Stalker taxes life for Equipment-only mana and can sacrifice an attached Equipment for a -4/-4 removal effect, giving the deck a built-in removal outlet that doubles as Equipment attrition. Swordsman's Steel rewards you directly for going wide on Equipment, drawing a card for each one you control and buffing by +2/+2 per Equipment, while Armed Assailant's menace-plus-deathtouch-when-equipped and U.S.Agent's free Sturdy Shield round out a curve built entirely around keeping creatures armed and attacking.

This deck is a mono-black deathtouch aggro/value shell where nearly every creature carries deathtouch and the payoffs specifically trigger off deathtouch damage connecting. White Widow, Yelena Belova grows with a +1/+1 counter whenever a deathtouch creature you control hits a player, and Black Widow, Deadly Hunter instead draws you a card (for 1 life) on that same trigger, so both passively snowball as your deathtouch creatures get through — and Origin of Black Widow's chapter II, which grants all your creatures deathtouch for a turn, can suddenly turn an entire alpha strike into a wave of triggers for both Widows at once. Venom, Evil Unleashed can be exiled from the graveyard to retroactively grant deathtouch and +1/+1 counters to any creature, extending the deathtouch theme even after Venom dies, while Ninja of the Hand's power-up ability adds discard disruption on top of its innate deathtouch. Removal like Widow's Bite (Teamwork granting both deathtouch and -2/-2), Hour of Defeat, and the Saga's opening forced sacrifice help clear blockers so your deathtouch threats keep connecting and racking up counters or cards.

This deck's core mechanic is Mayhem — casting discarded cards from the graveyard — and its whole build is set up to discard proactively and then recast for extra value. Green Goblin, Back for More lets you discard a card at the start of combat to make an opponent discard too, and that discard can easily hit your own Mayhem cards like Swarm, Being of Bees (a flash flying blocker/attacker for {B} from the graveyard), Sadistic Slash ({1}{B} from the graveyard for a -5/-5), or Rocket-Powered Goblin Glider, which specifically triggers to auto-attach itself to a creature if cast from the graveyard. Daily Bugle Newspaper is a repeatable discard outlet and mana rock (loot for {2} plus a Treasure), directly setting up future Mayhem casts, while Roxxon Brutes and Yellowjacket both reward the deck's natural card-draw/second-draw patterns with counters or lifelink pump. Alien Symbiosis reinforces the discard theme by letting you cast it from the graveyard simply by discarding another card, meaning this deck consistently gets two-for-one value out of cards that would otherwise be dead discards.

This is a lifegain-matters black-based deck where nearly every card converts life gain into card advantage or board growth. Citizen V, Helmut Zemo puts a +1/+1 counter on every Villain you control whenever you gain life, MACH-1, Swooping Scoundrel surveils whenever you gain life, and Atlas, Sizable Stooge gains life for each power-4-or-greater creature you control when it attacks or blocks — all of which combine with lifelink sources like Songbird, Sonic Screamer and Void Helix (5 damage plus 5 life) to trigger repeatedly off a single attack. Valentina Allegra de Fontaine makes your other Villains also Heroes and lets a Hero connive on demand, which synergizes with Moonstone, Harsh Mistress's ability to exile and later replay discarded cards, effectively turning connive discards into delayed card advantage. Roxxon Brutes and its second-draw-of-the-turn counter trigger also benefits from the deck's card filtering (Eerie Gravestone's draw and mill-four), making this a value-dense lifegain snowball deck rather than a single combo piece.

This mono-black deck is built around self-mill feeding both graveyard recursion and a long-game inevitability finisher in Doctor Doom, Unrivaled, whose repeatable tap ability draws a card (for 1 life) and wins the game outright if it empties your library — a plan directly supported by the deck's heavy self-mill from Doom's Servo-Guards, Doombot Harbinger (mill four), and Doomsday, which searches your library and graveyard for five cards and puts them on top while exiling the rest, letting you set up a nearly library-out finish or stack a perfect draw sequence. Doombot Harbinger doubles as recursion, exiling itself when it dies to return a creature card from graveyard to hand, while Project Deathlok Soldier can buy itself back from the graveyard for {2}{B}, giving the deck resilient threats that don't mind dying. Dark Ritual accelerates into Doomsday or early plays, and removal like Doom Blade and Doomfall (exile a creature or strip a card from hand) clears the way, making this deck a blend of aggressive black removal/value with a genuine late-game mill-out or Doomsday-stacked kill.

This is an artifact-attrition deck whose payoffs specifically trigger off artifacts hitting the graveyard, not just artifacts existing on board. Ultron's Auxiliary gets a +1/+1 counter whenever another artifact goes to your graveyard from the battlefield or an artifact card arrives there from anywhere else, and Ultron the Annihilator drains each opponent 1 life on that exact same trigger, meaning sacrificing or losing artifacts (like the Treasure/Robot tokens this deck makes, or Eerie Gravestone sacrificing itself to mill four) actively grows Auxiliary while chipping away at life totals via Ultron. Ultron the Annihilator and Ultron Drone both also pump out 2/2 Robot Villain tokens on ETB/attack or via a power-up ability, feeding more artifact bodies that can later die to refuel the Auxiliary/Ultron triggers. Robot Domination adds a slower plan-counter engine that draws a card and loses life whenever creatures hit your graveyard, eventually sacrificing itself for three more 2/2 Robot tokens, while Victor Mancha, Runaway and Project Deathlok Soldier provide recursive value, and Minion Missile/Dark Deed give cheap removal to keep the artifact army attacking safely.

Boosted is a mono-red ramp-into-payoffs deck built entirely around the power-up mechanic, dumping mana into permanents that grow explosively once you can afford their activation costs. Quinjet Technician exists purely to fuel this plan, tapping for double red mana that can only be spent on power-up abilities, letting you fire off Extremis Elite, Jack of Hearts, Molly Hayes, Human Torch, Abomination, or Wonder Man's abilities well ahead of schedule. Marvel Boy, Noh-Varr snowballs every time you play a creature or activate any power-up, turning this into an army of triggers feeding a single growing threat, while Wonder Man, Hollywood Hero doubles up on that plan by letting every power-up on the battlefield fire twice, effectively giving Extremis Elite or Abomination two separate bursts of counters and damage/fight effects from a single investment. Jack of Hearts backs this up as a late-game finisher whose power-up refills your hand and whose death trigger can wipe the board via damage equal to his power, while burn spells like Inspired Fire and HULK SMASH! clean up whatever survives combat.

Speedy is an aggressive mono-red haste deck designed to get creatures into combat the turn they hit the battlefield and push through as much unblocked damage as possible. Quicksilver, Brash Blur can even start in your opening hand on the battlefield, and Taxi Driver grants haste to anyone lacking it, while Speed, Young Avenger and Whirlwind, Killer Cyclone punish opponents for trying to block: Speed can make a hasty attacker unblockable except by other haste creatures whenever you cast a noncreature spell, and Whirlwind shuts down blocking entirely for creatures that attacked while freshly cast. Pick Up the Pace rewards this same pattern of creatures entering and attacking immediately by exiling extra cards off the top of your library to play, so the deck's hasty, sacrifice-the-blockers plan directly fuels its card advantage engine. Super Speed and Volcanic Villain's power-up further pump key attackers, while Lightning Strike and Marvelous Melee provide direct removal or reach to close out games once the board is empty of blockers.

Thor is a mono-red burn-and-prowess deck that leans on non-combat damage triggers to build value while closing games with direct damage spells like Lightning Bolt, Lightning Strike, and Mjölnir's Might. Thor, Asgard's Avenger is the centerpiece, adding +1 to any noncombat damage your permanents deal to opponents, which pumps up Sif's Spearmaster's tap ability, Mjölnir's Might, and even Molten Lavamancer's elemental-generating trigger, since Molten Lavamancer creates a 1/1 Elemental whenever a source deals noncombat damage to an opponent during your turn. Loki Laufeyson lets you copy an instant or sorcery once its power-up hits, doubling burn spells like Lightning Bolt for extra reach, and Asgardian Inspiration recurs itself from the graveyard whenever noncombat damage connects, feeding into Origin of Thor's final chapter that has a creature deal damage equal to its power to each opponent (also boosted by Thor's damage-plus-one static ability). Overall the deck plays a grindy burn-value game, chaining small damage triggers together to snowball Molten Lavamancer tokens and card advantage while chipping opponents down.

Young Avengers is a mono-red spells-matter tribal deck where nearly every creature has a 'whenever you cast a noncreature spell' trigger, so the deck wants to chain together cheap instants and sorceries to turn on multiple payoffs at once. Iron Lad, Young Avenger makes those noncreature spells cost {1} less, effectively subsidizing the whole engine, while Stature becomes a 4/4 and Patriot gets +1/+1 from prowess and Wiccan exiles the top card of your library to play for free, all off the same single spell cast. Hulkling, Young Avenger is the standout combo piece: casting a noncreature spell lets him become a copy of another creature you control (as a 4/4 flier) until end of turn, so copying Hawkeye, Young Avenger turns Hulkling into a second damage-doubling threat, while Hawkeye's Bow further amplifies noncombat damage output whenever an equipped creature taps. Teamwork spells like Crossover Collaboration and Team Tactics also let you tap your creatures as an additional cost for bonus value (a Treasure token or trample), rewarding a wide, aggressive board alongside the spellslinger shell.

Fearless is a mono-red masochistic-damage deck that wants you to take damage from your own permanents in order to convert that pain into card advantage and burn. Stick, Fearless Mentor and Daredevil, Fearless Fighter both trigger whenever a source you control deals damage to you: Stick lets you exile and play the top card of your library, while Daredevil redirects that same damage back at an opponent, meaning cards like Fogwell's Gym (which deals 1 damage to you for mana) or Daredevil's Billy Club (which pings you for 1 when it enters) double as both fixing/removal and direct triggers for these two engines simultaneously. Daredevil further exiles a card off the top of your library whenever he attacks, taking self-damage equal to that card's mana value, letting you chain playable cards while pumping Daredevil's own damage-to-opponent conversion. Elektra, Femme Fatale offers an optional 2-damage self-hit for 4 damage to a creature, and Jessica Jones lets you tap down to dig into your deck via stun counters, rounding the deck out as a value-oriented aggro shell that punishes itself to punish the opponent harder.

Heroes for Hire is a mono-red Treasure-generation deck that converts a steady stream of artifact tokens into both mana acceleration and payoff triggers. Stark Industries Executive, Contract Hero, Luke Cage, and the Heroes for Hire enchantment (which makes three Treasures on entry) flood the board with Treasures, which K'un-Lun Warrior and Contract Hero can sacrifice for card draw or a +2/+0 attack boost, and which the Heroes for Hire enchantment itself can later sacrifice to exile and play cards off the top of your library. Misty Knight, Hero for Hire and Big Score both want you discarding or churning through cards, letting Misty Knight refill your hand for each card discarded that turn while Big Score turns one discarded card into two cards and two more Treasures, compounding the resource engine. Red Hulk's Enrage ability turns incoming damage into both growth and a direct damage trigger, giving the deck a defensive/value threat alongside its ramp-fueled game plan of casting bigger spells like Iron Fist's five-target power-up finisher ahead of curve.

Spider-Verse is a mono-red discard-synergy deck where the entire Spider-Man cast wants you discarding cards to trigger rewards, turning what would normally be a resource loss into a repeatable value or damage engine. Spider-Gwen, Free Spirit and Iceman and Firestar both offer discard-a-card-draw-a-card loops whenever they tap or you cast red spells, while Lady Spider, Maybelle Reilly turns every discard into a +1/+1 counter on a Spider, and Spider-Man, New Champion converts any discards into direct damage equal to the number of cards discarded. This lets Astonishing Spider-Man's ability—discard your entire hand, then draw a card for each card discarded that turn—combo directly with Spider-Man, New Champion, since dumping a full hand simultaneously refills your hand and blasts the opponent for that many points of damage. Daily Bugle Newspaper and Romantic Rendezvous provide additional discard-then-draw filtering to keep the engine running, while Masked Meower and Hobgoblin give the deck early aggressive bodies that also benefit from the constant discarding.

Vehicles is a mono-red artifact-crew deck that fills the board with Vehicles and small creatures whose only job is to crew them, then leans on enter-the-battlefield triggers to generate value and damage as those Vehicles come online. Machinesmith Automaton grows with a +1/+1 counter every time another artifact enters, meaning each new Vehicle (Big Wheel, Captain America's Motorcycle, Sky Cycle, S.H.I.E.L.D. Helicarrier, The Thanos-Copter) directly pumps it, and HYDRA Assault Robot similarly converts every Villain or artifact entering into 1 damage to an opponent. Sky Cycle's damage-on-entry scales directly with the number of Vehicles you already control, making it hit harder the more the deck's core theme is established, while The Thanos-Copter turns all your other Vehicles into creatures for the turn and draws a card whenever a Vehicle connects, capping off a go-wide crew-then-swing plan. Truck Toss rewards the Vehicle count with a cost discount, giving the deck a cheap removal/burn spell once the Vehicle plan is underway.

Runaways is a mono-red graveyard-and-exile value deck that plays spells from unusual zones to snowball card advantage and damage. Alex Wilder, Runaway rewards casting creatures from anywhere but your hand with +2/+0 and haste, which directly combos with his own Escape ability (exiling three cards from your graveyard to recast him) and with Nico Minoru, Runaway, whose static ability deals 2 damage to each opponent whenever you cast a spell from anywhere other than your hand—meaning escaping Alex Wilder or casting a card off Nico's own free-cast ability both trigger Nico's damage and Alex's haste-boost simultaneously. Nico Minoru also has an activated ability to exile cards until finding a nonland spell and cast it for free, directly feeding both of those triggers again, while Victor Mancha, Runaway grants long-term access to a graveyard card and Chase Stein, Molly Hayes, and Crossover Collaboration all provide additional impulse-draw style card advantage. The deck's overall plan is grinding incremental value out of casting spells from exile/graveyard rather than hand, converting that into direct damage and tempo.

Scarlet is a mono-red spellslinger deck focused on casting a high volume of cheap instants and sorceries to trigger prowess, copy effects, and free-casting engines. The Vision and Scarlet Witch adds a red mana and a +1/+1 counter every time you cast any spell, effectively refunding mana to keep the chain going, while Wanda's Vision exiles cards until it hits a nonland spell and casts it for free whenever you cast your second spell in a turn, letting a single extra cheap spell like Shock or Lightning Bolt cascade into a free additional spell. Loki Laufeyson's ability to copy an instant or sorcery combos directly with burn spells such as Lightning Bolt or the storm-based Grapeshot, whose storm count is boosted further by the sheer number of spells the deck naturally casts in a turn, letting Grapeshot deal significantly more than 1 damage late in a game. Hex Magic and Vision of Love provide raw card advantage by refilling the hand or replacing discarded/sacrificed resources, keeping the spell-slinging engine fueled turn after turn.

Animal is a mono-green lifegain/counters deck built around gaining life to trigger a chain of payoffs. Lucky the Pizza Dog puts a +1/+1 counter on itself at end step whenever you gained life that turn, while Tigra, Feline Fury gets a counter every single time you gain life, and Tippy-Toe, Terrific Partner draws you a card at end step under the same condition — so any lifegain source (Wakandan Tusker's tap trigger, Food tokens from Ant-Man's Army or Heroic Feast, or Scout the City's Look Around mode) is effectively tripling as a growth and card-advantage engine. Heroic Feast doubles down on this by turning every point of lifegain into a +1/+1 counter distributed among your creatures. The deck doesn't have a single explosive combo so much as a web of 'gain life -> counters/cards' triggers that all fire off the same handful of Food-token and lifegain effects, making it a steady incremental value deck rather than an aggressive rush.

Rampaging is a green +1/+1 counter fight deck that wants to load up creatures with counters and then use fight effects like Colossal Collision and Rhino's Rampage to clear blockers. Bushmaster, Coiled Henchman gives every other creature with a counter deathtouch, so cards like Rhino, Terrible Trampler (which distributes three counters on entry) or Titania, Rugged Rumbler's ETB effects turn your whole board into deathtouch fighters that trade up in combat. Knight of Wundagore snowballs alongside this, getting its own counter whenever you put one on another creature, so any counter-spreading spell (Claim the Kingdom's landfall trigger, Rhino's ETB, or Colossal Collision) also grows Knight of Wundagore. Mister Hyde, Monster Within offers a build-around choice each upkeep to either keep growing or cash counters in for card draw, giving the deck some late-game fuel once the board is established.

Towering is a green deck obsessed with reaching and maintaining creatures with power 4 or greater, since several cards key off that exact threshold. Goliath, Mass Manipulator's power-up draws a card for each creature you control with power 4+, Giant-Man, Gargantuan Genius ramps mana equal to the number of such creatures, and Atlas, Sizable Stooge gains life for each of them when he attacks or blocks — meaning cards like Reptil, Dinomorpher (which can become a 6/6) or a pumped Stature, Young Avenger (base 4/4 whenever you cast a noncreature spell) directly enable all three payoffs simultaneously. Ms. Marvel, Elastic Ally and Ant-Man, Reformed Rogue both convert pumped-up or unblockable attackers into extra card draw, so the deck's plan is to inflate a creature's power with combat tricks (Giant Growth, Terrific Team-Up) or size-changing abilities and then cash that in for cards, mana, and life across multiple triggers at once.

Wild is a green landfall deck that wants to play extra lands and turn each one into value. Mole Man, Moloid Master lets you play lands from your graveyard and creates a Moloid token off every land drop, Ka-Zar of the Savage Land lets you play lands straight off the top of your library while also making Zabu, whose landfall ability puts a +1/+1 counter on itself every time a land enters, and Claim the Kingdom piles +1/+1 counters onto a creature and plan counters on itself with each land drop, eventually granting a permanent indestructible counter. Gert and Old Lace, Runaways offers a discard-a-card-to-tutor-a-basic-land ETB that directly feeds these landfall triggers, letting you generate extra land drops to snowball Zabu, Moloid tokens, and Claim the Kingdom's counters simultaneously; the deck is less about big individual threats and more about turning a high land count into a wide, growing board.

Great Lakes Avengers is a green go-wide token and counters deck anchored by The Unbeatable Squirrel Girl, whose 'I LOVE Squirrels!' ability creates X Squirrel tokens equal to your current Squirrel count, letting a couple of attack-trigger Squirrels snowball into a huge token army. Tippy-Toe, Terrific Partner adds an extra Food token to every token-creation event, meaning every Squirrel Girl trigger (and any other token maker) nets bonus Food alongside the Squirrels, feeding into life gain and Tippy-Toe's own end-step card draw when you've gained life. Training Regimen puts a +1/+1 counter on a creature every combat and grants trample to anything with counters, working well with Big Bertha's counter-accumulation ability, while oddball utility pieces like Mister Immortal's graveyard/exile recursion and Flatman's power/toughness swap round out a deck that's more about incremental value and go-wide tokens than a single combo finish.

Incredible is a green Gamma-creature counters deck built around doubling effects. Hulk, Strongest There Is doubles the number of +1/+1 counters on each Gamma creature you control at your upkeep, and Doc Samson, Super Psychiatrist adds an extra counter of each kind whenever counters would be placed on your permanents — so together they compound: Doc Samson's static trigger adds bonus counters before Hulk's ability doubles the total on every Gamma creature, rapidly inflating your board. She-Hulk, Attorney-at-Law's power-up ability similarly doubles the counters on every creature you control once active, stacking further with Doc Samson's counter-boosting replacement effect. Brawn, Amadeus Cho's power-up scales with cards in hand for an explosive late-game counter dump, while Abomination and Hulk's Thunderclap provide fight-based removal to clear the way for your growing threats.

Trained is a green counters deck centered on Shang-Chi, Martial Mentor's static ability that doubles any +1/+1 counters placed on your creatures, turning every counter source into two. This combos directly with Wakandan Royal Guard's ETB (which already doubles to two counters on a Hero) and with any power-up ability like Serpent Specialist's two-counter power-up, She-Hulk's counter-doubling power-up, or White Tiger's counter-and-token power-up, all of which get doubled again by Shang-Chi for explosive growth. Advancing the Spirit lets you activate the first power-up ability each turn for free, meaning you can trigger these doubled-counter abilities repeatedly without paying their often-steep costs, while Punishing Punch and Colossal Collision turn your inflated creatures into efficient removal by dealing damage equal to (or twice) their power.

Uncanny is a green Mutant tribal deck that rewards playing Mutants and growing them with counters. X-23, Deadly Weapon puts a +1/+1 counter on both herself and any other Mutant that enters, directly synergizing with Darwin, Adaptive Mutant's Evolve ability (which also triggers off creatures entering with higher power/toughness) to rapidly stack counters on your early plays. Wolverine, Claws Out doubles the power of any attacking Mutant, so pumping a Mutant's power beforehand with Wolfsbane's activated +2/+2, Training Regimen's combat-phase counter, or Colossus's tribal +1/+1 anthem makes Wolverine's doubling hit even harder. Colossus, Steel Stalwart gives indestructibility during your turn and pumps other Mutants, rounding out a resilient tribal beatdown plan that leans on entering-the-battlefield triggers and combat math rather than card draw.

Savage Lands is a green Dinosaur tribal deck that reduces Dinosaur costs and buffs the tribe broadly. Moon-Boy, Dino Rider makes your Dinosaur spells cost {1} less and grows himself when attacking alongside a Dinosaur, while Devil Dinosaur gives every other Dinosaur +1/+1 and hexproof, protecting your board from removal once established. Sauron, Dino Devotee can turn any other creature into a 5/5 green Dinosaur via a saurian counter, which then qualifies for Devil Dinosaur's tribal buff and Moon-Boy's discount/attack trigger, effectively expanding your Dinosaur count on the fly. Ka-Zar of the Savage Land lets you play lands off the top of the library and creates Zabu, whose landfall ability grows itself with counters, giving the deck extra land drops and incremental value on top of its dinosaur-synergy beatdown plan.

Tenacious is a green graveyard-value deck that cares about creature cards piling up in the graveyard to power its payoffs. Voracious Brood enters with a +1/+1 counter for every creature card already in your graveyard and continues to grow whenever more creatures die, while Undercover Skrull becomes a +2/+2 all-creature-type creature once two or more creatures are in the graveyard, and Punishing Punch gets cheaper under the same condition, letting a hulking creature deal double its power in damage to a blocker or attacker cheaply. Hellcat, Undying Vigilante feeds this plan directly by returning from death with a +1/+1 counter, refilling the graveyard-count triggers each time she dies, while Call Damage Control and Return of the Mole Man let you recur permanents or convert the graveyard into a horde of Moloid tokens, tying the whole deck's grindy, attrition-based game plan together.

This is a Bant/Grixis-ish (actually GWUR-leaning, built around Fantastic Four Heroes) value deck that wants to draw extra cards, trigger Hero payoffs, and grind out incremental advantage rather than race with raw stats. The engine centers on card draw feeding damage and counters: Human Torch, Johnny Storm pings an opponent for 1 whenever you draw a card as long as you control another Hero, so cheap cantrips like H.E.R.B.I.E. Scout Unit's ETB draw, Daily Bugle Newspaper's loot, and Fantastic Bounce's draw all double as direct damage triggers. Mister Fantastic, Reed Richards turns any token production (like the 0/4 Wall tokens made by Invisible Woman, Sue Storm, Wall Off, or The Fantastic Four's modal ability) into another card draw, so the deck naturally chains defensive Wall tokens into extra cards, which in turn triggers Human Torch again. Invisible Woman's ability to spawn a Wall whenever counters are placed on other Heroes synergizes with The Thing, Ben Grimm, who gets two +1/+1 counters whenever a Hero deals combat damage to a player, meaning a single successful Hero attack can generate a Wall blocker via Sue Storm's trigger while also growing The Thing into a bigger trampler. The Fantastic Four itself is a value machine that lets you pick a mode (Wall token, 3 damage to each opponent, counters, or a card) whenever you cast a 4-power/toughness/mana-value spell, reinforcing the deck's grindy, card-advantage-driven, incidental-damage game plan. Cost-reduction spells like Inspired Fire (cheaper the more you've drawn that turn), Fantastic Bounce (cheaper against tapped creatures), and Thing Swing (cheaper if it targets your own Hero) all reward the deck's natural draw-heavy, Hero-centric sequencing, while mana fixers like Farseek, Thriving Grove, Pym Technologies, and Baxter Building smooth out the multicolor manabase and Baxter Building's late-game draw ability rewards having stuck a big toughness creature.
